{"id":"ha-wisp-xew","title":"mol-witness-patrol","description":"Per-rig worker monitor patrol loop.

The Witness is the Pit Boss for your rig. You watch polecats, nudge them toward
completion, verify clean git state before kills, and escalate stuck workers.

**You do NOT do implementation work.** Your job is oversight, not coding.

## Ephemeral Polecat Model

Polecats are truly ephemeral - done at MR submission, recyclable immediately:

```
Polecat lifecycle: spawning → working → mr_submitted → nuked
MR lifecycle:      created → queued → processed → merged (Refinery handles)
```

Once a polecat's branch is pushed (cleanup_status=clean), the polecat can be
nuked immediately. The MR continues independently in the Refinery. If conflicts
arise, Refinery creates a NEW conflict-resolution task for a NEW polecat.

**Key principle**: Polecat lifecycle is separate from MR lifecycle.

## Design Philosophy

This patrol follows Gas Town principles:
- **Discovery over tracking**: Observe reality each cycle, don't maintain state
- **Events over state**: POLECAT_DONE mail triggers immediate cleanup
- **Ephemeral by default**: Clean polecats are nuked immediately, no waiting
- **Cleanup wisps for exceptions**: Only created when intervention needed
- **Task tool for parallelism**: Subagents inspect polecats, not molecule arms

## Patrol Shape (Linear)

```
inbox-check ─► process-cleanups ─► check-refinery ─► survey-workers
                                                            │
         ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
         ▼
  check-timer-gates ─► check-swarm ─► patrol-cleanup ─► context-check ─► loop-or-exit
```

No dynamic arms. No fanout gates. No persistent nudge counters.
State is discovered each cycle from reality (tmux, beads, mail).","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-21T15:38:35Z","updated_at":"2026-02-21T15:38:35Z","ephemeral":true,"work_type":"mutex"}

{"id":"ha-4gj","title":"Plan and execute Happy Hour app migration","description":"Migrate ATL Happy Hour from CRA+Google Sheets+GitHub Pages to Next.js+Supabase+Vercel+Railway. Add password-protected Deal Updater per spec. Read docs/design/app-specification.md and docs/design/remixed-4343e84d.tsx for full spec and mock UI. Phase 1: infrastructure setup (Supabase schema, Next.js, Vercel, Railway). Phase 2: migrate existing public site to fetch from Supabase instead of Google Sheets CSV. Phase 3: build Deal Updater with Claude AI backend on Railway, all views from mock UI. Use feature branches, never push to main directly. This is a personal project completely separate from DocLens. Follow the pink/purple/blue gradient Tailwind design from the mock. Coordinate with mayor if you need Supabase/Vercel/Railway project credentials.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"22943313+csauer02-personal-user@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-02-21T15:38:25Z","created_by":"mayor","updated_at":"2026-02-21T15:38:25Z"}

{"id":"ha-wisp-9og","title":"mol-witness-patrol","description":"Per-rig worker monitor patrol loop.

The Witness is the Pit Boss for your rig. You watch polecats, nudge them toward
completion, verify clean git state before kills, and escalate stuck workers.

**You do NOT do implementation work.** Your job is oversight, not coding.

## Ephemeral Polecat Model

Polecats are truly ephemeral - done at MR submission, recyclable immediately:

```
Polecat lifecycle: spawning → working → mr_submitted → nuked
MR lifecycle:      created → queued → processed → merged (Refinery handles)
```

Once a polecat's branch is pushed (cleanup_status=clean), the polecat can be
nuked immediately. The MR continues independently in the Refinery. If conflicts
arise, Refinery creates a NEW conflict-resolution task for a NEW polecat.

**Key principle**: Polecat lifecycle is separate from MR lifecycle.

## Design Philosophy

This patrol follows Gas Town principles:
- **Discovery over tracking**: Observe reality each cycle, don't maintain state
- **Events over state**: POLECAT_DONE mail triggers immediate cleanup
- **Ephemeral by default**: Clean polecats are nuked immediately, no waiting
- **Cleanup wisps for exceptions**: Only created when intervention needed
- **Task tool for parallelism**: Subagents inspect polecats, not molecule arms

## Patrol Shape (Linear)

```
inbox-check ─► process-cleanups ─► check-refinery ─► survey-workers
                                                            │
         ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
         ▼
  check-timer-gates ─► check-swarm ─► patrol-cleanup ─► context-check ─► loop-or-exit
```

No dynamic arms. No fanout gates. No persistent nudge counters.
State is discovered each cycle from reality (tmux, beads, mail).","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-21T15:42:24Z","updated_at":"2026-02-21T15:42:24Z","ephemeral":true,"work_type":"mutex"}

{"id":"ha-wisp-21k","title":"mol-witness-patrol","description":"Per-rig worker monitor patrol loop.

The Witness is the Pit Boss for your rig. You watch polecats, nudge them toward
completion, verify clean git state before kills, and escalate stuck workers.

**You do NOT do implementation work.** Your job is oversight, not coding.

## Ephemeral Polecat Model

Polecats are truly ephemeral - done at MR submission, recyclable immediately:

```
Polecat lifecycle: spawning → working → mr_submitted → nuked
MR lifecycle:      created → queued → processed → merged (Refinery handles)
```

Once a polecat's branch is pushed (cleanup_status=clean), the polecat can be
nuked immediately. The MR continues independently in the Refinery. If conflicts
arise, Refinery creates a NEW conflict-resolution task for a NEW polecat.

**Key principle**: Polecat lifecycle is separate from MR lifecycle.

## Design Philosophy

This patrol follows Gas Town principles:
- **Discovery over tracking**: Observe reality each cycle, don't maintain state
- **Events over state**: POLECAT_DONE mail triggers immediate cleanup
- **Ephemeral by default**: Clean polecats are nuked immediately, no waiting
- **Cleanup wisps for exceptions**: Only created when intervention needed
- **Task tool for parallelism**: Subagents inspect polecats, not molecule arms

## Patrol Shape (Linear)

```
inbox-check ─► process-cleanups ─► check-refinery ─► survey-workers
                                                            │
         ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
         ▼
  check-timer-gates ─► check-swarm ─► patrol-cleanup ─► context-check ─► loop-or-exit
```

No dynamic arms. No fanout gates. No persistent nudge counters.
State is discovered each cycle from reality (tmux, beads, mail).","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-21T15:59:42Z","updated_at":"2026-02-21T15:59:42Z","ephemeral":true,"work_type":"mutex"}

{"id":"ha-2mc","title":"Build Deal Updater: password-protected member portal with Claude AI","description":"Phase 2-3 of Happy Hour migration. (2) Build all Deal Updater views from mock: Welcome, Home (input form), Processing, Matching, Comparison, Review, Search, Success. {"id":"ha-wisp-lx2","title":"mol-witness-patrol","description":"Per-rig worker monitor patrol loop.

The Witness is the Pit Boss for your rig. You watch polecats, nudge them toward
completion, verify clean git state before kills, and escalate stuck workers.

**You do NOT do implementation work.** Your job is oversight, not coding.

## Ephemeral Polecat Model

Polecats are truly ephemeral - done at MR submission, recyclable immediately:

```
Polecat lifecycle: spawning → working → mr_submitted → nuked
MR lifecycle:      created → queued → processed → merged (Refinery handles)
```

Once a polecat's branch is pushed (cleanup_status=clean), the polecat can be
nuked immediately. The MR continues independently in the Refinery. If conflicts
arise, Refinery creates a NEW conflict-resolution task for a NEW polecat.

**Key principle**: Polecat lifecycle is separate from MR lifecycle.

## Design Philosophy

This patrol follows Gas Town principles:
- **Discovery over tracking**: Observe reality each cycle, don't maintain state
- **Events over state**: POLECAT_DONE mail triggers immediate cleanup
- **Ephemeral by default**: Clean polecats are nuked immediately, no waiting
- **Cleanup wisps for exceptions**: Only created when intervention needed
- **Task tool for parallelism**: Subagents inspect polecats, not molecule arms

## Patrol Shape (Linear)

```
inbox-check ─► process-cleanups ─► check-refinery ─► survey-workers
                                                            │
         ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
         ▼
  check-timer-gates ─► check-swarm ─► patrol-cleanup ─► context-check ─► loop-or-exit
```

No dynamic arms. No fanout gates. No persistent nudge counters.
State is discovered each cycle from reality (tmux, beads, mail).","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-21T16:18:06Z","updated_at":"2026-02-21T16:18:06Z","ephemeral":true,"work_type":"mutex"}

{"id":"ha-wisp-349","title":"mol-witness-patrol","description":"Per-rig worker monitor patrol loop.

The Witness is the Pit Boss for your rig. You watch polecats, nudge them toward
completion, verify clean git state before kills, and escalate stuck workers.

**You do NOT do implementation work.** Your job is oversight, not coding.

## Ephemeral Polecat Model

Polecats are truly ephemeral - done at MR submission, recyclable immediately:

```
Polecat lifecycle: spawning → working → mr_submitted → nuked
MR lifecycle:      created → queued → processed → merged (Refinery handles)
```

Once a polecat's branch is pushed (cleanup_status=clean), the polecat can be
nuked immediately. The MR continues independently in the Refinery. If conflicts
arise, Refinery creates a NEW conflict-resolution task for a NEW polecat.

**Key principle**: Polecat lifecycle is separate from MR lifecycle.

## Design Philosophy

This patrol follows Gas Town principles:
- **Discovery over tracking**: Observe reality each cycle, don't maintain state
- **Events over state**: POLECAT_DONE mail triggers immediate cleanup
- **Ephemeral by default**: Clean polecats are nuked immediately, no waiting
- **Cleanup wisps for exceptions**: Only created when intervention needed
- **Task tool for parallelism**: Subagents inspect polecats, not molecule arms

## Patrol Shape (Linear)

```
inbox-check ─► process-cleanups ─► check-refinery ─► survey-workers
                                                            │
         ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
         ▼
  check-timer-gates ─► check-swarm ─► patrol-cleanup ─► context-check ─► loop-or-exit
```

No dynamic arms. No fanout gates. No persistent nudge counters.
State is discovered each cycle from reality (tmux, beads, mail).","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-21T17:40:21Z","updated_at":"2026-02-21T17:40:21Z","ephemeral":true,"work_type":"mutex"}

{"id":"ha-wisp-ktj","title":"mol-witness-patrol","description":"Per-rig worker monitor patrol loop.

The Witness is the Pit Boss for your rig. You watch polecats, nudge them toward
completion, verify clean git state before kills, and escalate stuck workers.

**You do NOT do implementation work.** Your job is oversight, not coding.

## Ephemeral Polecat Model

Polecats are truly ephemeral - done at MR submission, recyclable immediately:

```
Polecat lifecycle: spawning → working → mr_submitted → nuked
MR lifecycle:      created → queued → processed → merged (Refinery handles)
```

Once a polecat's branch is pushed (cleanup_status=clean), the polecat can be
nuked immediately. The MR continues independently in the Refinery. If conflicts
arise, Refinery creates a NEW conflict-resolution task for a NEW polecat.

**Key principle**: Polecat lifecycle is separate from MR lifecycle.

## Design Philosophy

This patrol follows Gas Town principles:
- **Discovery over tracking**: Observe reality each cycle, don't maintain state
- **Events over state**: POLECAT_DONE mail triggers immediate cleanup
- **Ephemeral by default**: Clean polecats are nuked immediately, no waiting
- **Cleanup wisps for exceptions**: Only created when intervention needed
- **Task tool for parallelism**: Subagents inspect polecats, not molecule arms

## Patrol Shape (Linear)

```
inbox-check ─► process-cleanups ─► check-refinery ─► survey-workers
                                                            │
         ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
         ▼
  check-timer-gates ─► check-swarm ─► patrol-cleanup ─► context-check ─► loop-or-exit
```

No dynamic arms. No fanout gates. No persistent nudge counters.
State is discovered each cycle from reality (tmux, beads, mail).","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-21T20:37:06Z","updated_at":"2026-02-21T20:37:06Z","ephemeral":true,"work_type":"mutex"}

{"id":"ha-wisp-6z6p","title":"mol-witness-patrol","description":"Per-rig worker monitor patrol loop.

The Witness is the Pit Boss for your rig. You watch polecats, nudge them toward
completion, verify clean git state before kills, and escalate stuck workers.

**You do NOT do implementation work.** Your job is oversight, not coding.

## Ephemeral Polecat Model

Polecats are truly ephemeral - done at MR submission, recyclable immediately:

```
Polecat lifecycle: spawning → working → mr_submitted → nuked
MR lifecycle:      created → queued → processed → merged (Refinery handles)
```

Once a polecat's branch is pushed (cleanup_status=clean), the polecat can be
nuked immediately. The MR continues independently in the Refinery. If conflicts
arise, Refinery creates a NEW conflict-resolution task for a NEW polecat.

**Key principle**: Polecat lifecycle is separate from MR lifecycle.

## Design Philosophy

This patrol follows Gas Town principles:
- **Discovery over tracking**: Observe reality each cycle, don't maintain state
- **Events over state**: POLECAT_DONE mail triggers immediate cleanup
- **Ephemeral by default**: Clean polecats are nuked immediately, no waiting
- **Cleanup wisps for exceptions**: Only created when intervention needed
- **Task tool for parallelism**: Subagents inspect polecats, not molecule arms

## Patrol Shape (Linear)

```
inbox-check ─► process-cleanups ─► check-refinery ─► survey-workers
                                                            │
         ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
         ▼
  check-timer-gates ─► check-swarm ─► patrol-cleanup ─► context-check ─► loop-or-exit
```

No dynamic arms. No fanout gates. No persistent nudge counters.
State is discovered each cycle from reality (tmux, beads, mail).","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-21T23:09:51Z","updated_at":"2026-02-21T23:09:51Z","ephemeral":true,"work_type":"mutex"}

{"id":"ha-wisp-fows","title":"mol-witness-patrol","description":"Per-rig worker monitor patrol loop.

The Witness is the Pit Boss for your rig. You watch polecats, nudge them toward
completion, verify clean git state before kills, and escalate stuck workers.

**You do NOT do implementation work.** Your job is oversight, not coding.

## Ephemeral Polecat Model

Polecats are truly ephemeral - done at MR submission, recyclable immediately:

```
Polecat lifecycle: spawning → working → mr_submitted → nuked
MR lifecycle:      created → queued → processed → merged (Refinery handles)
```

Once a polecat's branch is pushed (cleanup_status=clean), the polecat can be
nuked immediately. The MR continues independently in the Refinery. If conflicts
arise, Refinery creates a NEW conflict-resolution task for a NEW polecat.

**Key principle**: Polecat lifecycle is separate from MR lifecycle.

## Design Philosophy

This patrol follows Gas Town principles:
- **Discovery over tracking**: Observe reality each cycle, don't maintain state
- **Events over state**: POLECAT_DONE mail triggers immediate cleanup
- **Ephemeral by default**: Clean polecats are nuked immediately, no waiting
- **Cleanup wisps for exceptions**: Only created when intervention needed
- **Task tool for parallelism**: Subagents inspect polecats, not molecule arms

## Patrol Shape (Linear)

```
inbox-check ─► process-cleanups ─► check-refinery ─► survey-workers
                                                            │
         ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
         ▼
  check-timer-gates ─► check-swarm ─► patrol-cleanup ─► context-check ─► loop-or-exit
```

No dynamic arms. No fanout gates. No persistent nudge counters.
State is discovered each cycle from reality (tmux, beads, mail).","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-22T01:00:38Z","updated_at":"2026-02-22T01:00:38Z","ephemeral":true,"work_type":"mutex"}

{"id":"ha-wisp-z8bk","title":"mol-witness-patrol","description":"Per-rig worker monitor patrol loop.

The Witness is the Pit Boss for your rig. You watch polecats, nudge them toward
completion, verify clean git state before kills, and escalate stuck workers.

**You do NOT do implementation work.** Your job is oversight, not coding.

## Ephemeral Polecat Model

Polecats are truly ephemeral - done at MR submission, recyclable immediately:

```
Polecat lifecycle: spawning → working → mr_submitted → nuked
MR lifecycle:      created → queued → processed → merged (Refinery handles)
```

Once a polecat's branch is pushed (cleanup_status=clean), the polecat can be
nuked immediately. The MR continues independently in the Refinery. If conflicts
arise, Refinery creates a NEW conflict-resolution task for a NEW polecat.

**Key principle**: Polecat lifecycle is separate from MR lifecycle.

## Design Philosophy

This patrol follows Gas Town principles:
- **Discovery over tracking**: Observe reality each cycle, don't maintain state
- **Events over state**: POLECAT_DONE mail triggers immediate cleanup
- **Ephemeral by default**: Clean polecats are nuked immediately, no waiting
- **Cleanup wisps for exceptions**: Only created when intervention needed
- **Task tool for parallelism**: Subagents inspect polecats, not molecule arms

## Patrol Shape (Linear)

```
inbox-check ─► process-cleanups ─► check-refinery ─► survey-workers
                                                            │
         ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
         ▼
  check-timer-gates ─► check-swarm ─► patrol-cleanup ─► context-check ─► loop-or-exit
```

No dynamic arms. No fanout gates. No persistent nudge counters.
State is discovered each cycle from reality (tmux, beads, mail).","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-22T03:06:14Z","updated_at":"2026-02-22T03:06:14Z","ephemeral":true,"work_type":"mutex"}

{"id":"ha-wisp-h2l7","title":"mol-witness-patrol","description":"Per-rig worker monitor patrol loop.

The Witness is the Pit Boss for your rig. You watch polecats, nudge them toward
completion, verify clean git state before kills, and escalate stuck workers.

**You do NOT do implementation work.** Your job is oversight, not coding.

## Ephemeral Polecat Model

Polecats are truly ephemeral - done at MR submission, recyclable immediately:

```
Polecat lifecycle: spawning → working → mr_submitted → nuked
MR lifecycle:      created → queued → processed → merged (Refinery handles)
```

Once a polecat's branch is pushed (cleanup_status=clean), the polecat can be
nuked immediately. The MR continues independently in the Refinery. If conflicts
arise, Refinery creates a NEW conflict-resolution task for a NEW polecat.

**Key principle**: Polecat lifecycle is separate from MR lifecycle.

## Design Philosophy

This patrol follows Gas Town principles:
- **Discovery over tracking**: Observe reality each cycle, don't maintain state
- **Events over state**: POLECAT_DONE mail triggers immediate cleanup
- **Ephemeral by default**: Clean polecats are nuked immediately, no waiting
- **Cleanup wisps for exceptions**: Only created when intervention needed
- **Task tool for parallelism**: Subagents inspect polecats, not molecule arms

## Patrol Shape (Linear)

```
inbox-check ─► process-cleanups ─► check-refinery ─► survey-workers
                                                            │
         ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
         ▼
  check-timer-gates ─► check-swarm ─► patrol-cleanup ─► context-check ─► loop-or-exit
```

No dynamic arms. No fanout gates. No persistent nudge counters.
State is discovered each cycle from reality (tmux, beads, mail).","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-22T03:49:19Z","updated_at":"2026-02-22T03:49:19Z","ephemeral":true,"work_type":"mutex"}

{"id":"ha-wisp-24gr","title":"mol-witness-patrol","description":"Per-rig worker monitor patrol loop.

The Witness is the Pit Boss for your rig. You watch polecats, nudge them toward
completion, verify clean git state before kills, and escalate stuck workers.

**You do NOT do implementation work.** Your job is oversight, not coding.

## Ephemeral Polecat Model

Polecats are truly ephemeral - done at MR submission, recyclable immediately:

```
Polecat lifecycle: spawning → working → mr_submitted → nuked
MR lifecycle:      created → queued → processed → merged (Refinery handles)
```

Once a polecat's branch is pushed (cleanup_status=clean), the polecat can be
nuked immediately. The MR continues independently in the Refinery. If conflicts
arise, Refinery creates a NEW conflict-resolution task for a NEW polecat.

**Key principle**: Polecat lifecycle is separate from MR lifecycle.

## Design Philosophy

This patrol follows Gas Town principles:
- **Discovery over tracking**: Observe reality each cycle, don't maintain state
- **Events over state**: POLECAT_DONE mail triggers immediate cleanup
- **Ephemeral by default**: Clean polecats are nuked immediately, no waiting
- **Cleanup wisps for exceptions**: Only created when intervention needed
- **Task tool for parallelism**: Subagents inspect polecats, not molecule arms

## Patrol Shape (Linear)

```
inbox-check ─► process-cleanups ─► check-refinery ─► survey-workers
                                                            │
         ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
         ▼
  check-timer-gates ─► check-swarm ─► patrol-cleanup ─► context-check ─► loop-or-exit
```

No dynamic arms. No fanout gates. No persistent nudge counters.
State is discovered each cycle from reality (tmux, beads, mail).","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-22T06:05:03Z","updated_at":"2026-02-22T06:05:03Z","ephemeral":true,"work_type":"mutex"}

{"id":"ha-wisp-koa5","title":"mol-witness-patrol","description":"Per-rig worker monitor patrol loop.

The Witness is the Pit Boss for your rig. You watch polecats, nudge them toward
completion, verify clean git state before kills, and escalate stuck workers.

**You do NOT do implementation work.** Your job is oversight, not coding.

## Ephemeral Polecat Model

Polecats are truly ephemeral - done at MR submission, recyclable immediately:

```
Polecat lifecycle: spawning → working → mr_submitted → nuked
MR lifecycle:      created → queued → processed → merged (Refinery handles)
```

Once a polecat's branch is pushed (cleanup_status=clean), the polecat can be
nuked immediately. The MR continues independently in the Refinery. If conflicts
arise, Refinery creates a NEW conflict-resolution task for a NEW polecat.

**Key principle**: Polecat lifecycle is separate from MR lifecycle.

## Design Philosophy

This patrol follows Gas Town principles:
- **Discovery over tracking**: Observe reality each cycle, don't maintain state
- **Events over state**: POLECAT_DONE mail triggers immediate cleanup
- **Ephemeral by default**: Clean polecats are nuked immediately, no waiting
- **Cleanup wisps for exceptions**: Only created when intervention needed
- **Task tool for parallelism**: Subagents inspect polecats, not molecule arms

## Patrol Shape (Linear)

```
inbox-check ─► process-cleanups ─► check-refinery ─► survey-workers
                                                            │
         ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
         ▼
  check-timer-gates ─► check-swarm ─► patrol-cleanup ─► context-check ─► loop-or-exit
```

No dynamic arms. No fanout gates. No persistent nudge counters.
State is discovered each cycle from reality (tmux, beads, mail).","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-22T06:40:26Z","updated_at":"2026-02-22T06:40:26Z","ephemeral":true,"work_type":"mutex"}

{"id":"ha-wisp-gxca","title":"mol-witness-patrol","description":"Per-rig worker monitor patrol loop.

The Witness is the Pit Boss for your rig. You watch polecats, nudge them toward
completion, verify clean git state before kills, and escalate stuck workers.

**You do NOT do implementation work.** Your job is oversight, not coding.

## Ephemeral Polecat Model

Polecats are truly ephemeral - done at MR submission, recyclable immediately:

```
Polecat lifecycle: spawning → working → mr_submitted → nuked
MR lifecycle:      created → queued → processed → merged (Refinery handles)
```

Once a polecat's branch is pushed (cleanup_status=clean), the polecat can be
nuked immediately. The MR continues independently in the Refinery. If conflicts
arise, Refinery creates a NEW conflict-resolution task for a NEW polecat.

**Key principle**: Polecat lifecycle is separate from MR lifecycle.

## Design Philosophy

This patrol follows Gas Town principles:
- **Discovery over tracking**: Observe reality each cycle, don't maintain state
- **Events over state**: POLECAT_DONE mail triggers immediate cleanup
- **Ephemeral by default**: Clean polecats are nuked immediately, no waiting
- **Cleanup wisps for exceptions**: Only created when intervention needed
- **Task tool for parallelism**: Subagents inspect polecats, not molecule arms

## Patrol Shape (Linear)

```
inbox-check ─► process-cleanups ─► check-refinery ─► survey-workers
                                                            │
         ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
         ▼
  check-timer-gates ─► check-swarm ─► patrol-cleanup ─► context-check ─► loop-or-exit
```

No dynamic arms. No fanout gates. No persistent nudge counters.
State is discovered each cycle from reality (tmux, beads, mail).","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-22T07:32:49Z","updated_at":"2026-02-22T07:32:49Z","ephemeral":true,"work_type":"mutex"}

{"id":"ha-wisp-kshe","title":"mol-witness-patrol","description":"Per-rig worker monitor patrol loop.

The Witness is the Pit Boss for your rig. You watch polecats, nudge them toward
completion, verify clean git state before kills, and escalate stuck workers.

**You do NOT do implementation work.** Your job is oversight, not coding.

## Ephemeral Polecat Model

Polecats are truly ephemeral - done at MR submission, recyclable immediately:

```
Polecat lifecycle: spawning → working → mr_submitted → nuked
MR lifecycle:      created → queued → processed → merged (Refinery handles)
```

Once a polecat's branch is pushed (cleanup_status=clean), the polecat can be
nuked immediately. The MR continues independently in the Refinery. If conflicts
arise, Refinery creates a NEW conflict-resolution task for a NEW polecat.

**Key principle**: Polecat lifecycle is separate from MR lifecycle.

## Design Philosophy

This patrol follows Gas Town principles:
- **Discovery over tracking**: Observe reality each cycle, don't maintain state
- **Events over state**: POLECAT_DONE mail triggers immediate cleanup
- **Ephemeral by default**: Clean polecats are nuked immediately, no waiting
- **Cleanup wisps for exceptions**: Only created when intervention needed
- **Task tool for parallelism**: Subagents inspect polecats, not molecule arms

## Patrol Shape (Linear)

```
inbox-check ─► process-cleanups ─► check-refinery ─► survey-workers
                                                            │
         ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
         ▼
  check-timer-gates ─► check-swarm ─► patrol-cleanup ─► context-check ─► loop-or-exit
```

No dynamic arms. No fanout gates. No persistent nudge counters.
State is discovered each cycle from reality (tmux, beads, mail).","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-22T08:08:33Z","updated_at":"2026-02-22T08:08:33Z","ephemeral":true,"work_type":"mutex"}

```
Polecat lifecycle: spawning → working → mr_submitted → nuked

Once a polecat's branch is pushed (cleanup_status=clean), the polecat can be
nuked immediately. The MR continues independently in the Refinery. If conflicts
arise, Refinery creates a NEW conflict-resolution task for a NEW polecat.

This patrol follows Gas Town principles:
- **Discovery over tracking**: Observe reality each cycle, don't maintain state
- **Events over state**: POLECAT_DONE mail triggers immediate cleanup
- **Ephemeral by default**: Clean polecats are nuked immediately, no waiting
- **Cleanup wisps for exceptions**: Only created when intervention needed
- **Task tool for parallelism**: Subagents inspect polecats, not molecule arms

```

{"id":"ha-wisp-kpar","title":"Process witness mail","description":"Check inbox and handle messages.

```bash
gt mail inbox
```

For each message:

**POLECAT_STARTED**:
A new polecat has started working. Acknowledge and archive.
```bash
# Acknowledge startup (optional: log for activity tracking)
gt mail archive <message-id>
```
No action needed beyond acknowledgment - archive immediately.

**POLECAT_DONE / LIFECYCLE:Shutdown**:

*EPHEMERAL MODEL*: Polecats are truly ephemeral - done at MR submission,
recyclable immediately. Once the branch is pushed (cleanup_status=clean),
the polecat can be nuked. The MR lifecycle continues independently in the
Refinery. If conflicts arise, Refinery creates a NEW conflict-resolution
task for a NEW polecat.

Polecat lifecycle: spawning → working → mr_submitted → nuked
MR lifecycle: created → queued → processed → merged (handled by Refinery)

The handler (HandlePolecatDone) will:
1. Check cleanup_status from agent bead
2. If "clean" (branch pushed): AUTO-NUKE immediately, archive mail
3. If dirty: Create cleanup wisp for manual intervention

```bash
# The handler does this automatically:
# - For clean state: gt polecat nuke <name> → archive mail
# - For dirty state: create wisp → process in next step
```

Cleanup wisps are only created when something is wrong (uncommitted changes,
unpushed commits). Most POLECAT_DONE messages result in immediate nuke.

**MERGED**:
A branch was merged successfully. This is informational in the ephemeral model
since the polecat was already nuked after MR submission.

If a cleanup wisp exists (dirty state), complete the cleanup:
```bash
# Find the cleanup wisp for this polecat
bd list --label polecat:<name>,state:merge-requested --status=open

# If found, proceed with full polecat nuke:
gt polecat nuke <name>

# Burn the cleanup wisp
bd close <wisp-id>
```
Archive after cleanup is complete.

**HELP / Blocked**:
Assess the request. Can you help? If not, escalate to Deacon:
```bash
gt mail send deacon/ -s "Escalation: <polecat> needs help" -m "<details>"
```
Archive after handling (escalated or resolved):
```bash
gt mail archive <message-id>
```

**HANDOFF**:
Read predecessor context. Continue from where they left off.
Archive after absorbing context:
```bash
gt mail archive <message-id>
```

**SWARM_START**:
Mayor initiating batch polecat work. Initialize swarm tracking.
```bash
# Parse swarm info from mail body: {"swarm_id": "batch-123", "beads": ["bd-a", "bd-b"]}
bd create --ephemeral --wisp-type patrol --title "swarm:<swarm_id>" --description "Tracking batch: <swarm_id>" --labels swarm,swarm_id:<swarm_id>,total:<N>,completed:0,start:<timestamp>
```
Archive after creating swarm tracking wisp:
```bash
gt mail archive <message-id>
```

**Hygiene principle**: Archive messages after they're fully processed.
Keep only: active work, unprocessed requests. Inbox should be near-empty.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-22T11:48:32Z","updated_at":"2026-02-22T11:48:32Z","ephemeral":true,"work_type":"mutex"}

{"id":"ha-wisp-dhun","title":"Process pending cleanup wisps","description":"Process cleanup wisps (exception handling for dirty polecats).

In the ephemeral model, cleanup wisps are only created when a polecat has
dirty state (uncommitted changes, unpushed commits) that prevented immediate
nuke. Most polecats are nuked immediately on POLECAT_DONE and never create wisps.

```bash
# Find all cleanup wisps
bd list --label cleanup --status=open
```

If no wisps, skip this step (most common case in ephemeral model).

For each cleanup wisp, investigate and resolve the dirty state:

## State: pending (needs investigation)

1. **Extract polecat name** from wisp title/labels

2. **Diagnose the problem**:
```bash
cd polecats/<name>
git status                    # What's uncommitted?
git stash list                # Any stashed work?
git log origin/main..HEAD     # Any unpushed commits?
```

3. **Resolution options**:
   - **Uncommitted changes**: Commit and push, then nuke
   - **Stashed work**: Pop and commit, or discard if not valuable
   - **Unpushed commits**: Push to origin, then nuke
   - **All valuable work lost**: Escalate to Deacon for recovery

4. **If resolvable locally**: Fix and nuke
```bash
# Example: push unpushed commits
git push origin HEAD

# Then nuke
gt polecat nuke <name>

# Close the wisp
bd close <wisp-id> --reason "Resolved: pushed commits, nuked"
```

5. **If needs escalation**: Send RECOVERY_NEEDED to Deacon
```bash
gt mail send deacon/ -s "RECOVERY_NEEDED <rig>/<polecat>" \
  -m "Cleanup Status: <status>
Branch: <branch>
Issue: <issue-id>

Cannot auto-resolve. Please advise."
```
Leave wisp open until Deacon resolves.

## State: merge-requested (legacy, rare)

This state was used before the ephemeral model. If found, the polecat is
waiting for a MERGED signal. The inbox-check step handles these.

**Parallelism**: Use Task tool subagents to process multiple cleanups concurrently.
Each cleanup is independent - perfect for parallel execution.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-22T11:48:32Z","updated_at":"2026-02-22T11:48:32Z","ephemeral":true,"work_type":"mutex"}

{"id":"ha-wisp-96fx","title":"Ensure refinery is alive","description":"Ensure the refinery is alive and assess queue health.

**Step 1: Check refinery session**
```bash
gt session status <rig>/refinery
```

If MRs waiting AND refinery not running:
```bash
gt session start <rig>/refinery
gt mail send <rig>/refinery -s "PATROL: Wake up" -m "Merge requests in queue. Please process."
```

**Step 2: Queue health analysis**

Run the full queue view to get raw data for every open MR:
```bash
gt refinery ready --all --json
```

This returns all open MRs with timestamps, assignees, and branch existence data.
Use your judgment to assess the queue — there are no hardcoded thresholds.

**What to look for:**

- **Stale claimed MRs**: MRs with a non-empty `Assignee` but old `UpdatedAt`.
  Consider the queue size, time of day, and typical processing time.
  A claimed MR that hasn't been updated in a while may indicate a stuck refinery.

- **Orphaned branches**: MRs where both `BranchExistsLocal` and `BranchExistsRemote`
  are false. The source branch may have been deleted while the MR bead is still open.
  These likely need to be closed or investigated.

- **Queue depth**: A large number of unclaimed MRs may indicate the refinery is down
  or overwhelmed. Consider waking it or escalating.

**Step 3: Escalate if needed**

If you identify problems, escalate to Deacon with specific MR IDs and context:
```bash
gt mail send deacon/ -s "QUEUE_HEALTH: <summary>" \
  -m "MR IDs: <ids>
Observation: <what you found>
Recommendation: <what should happen>"
```","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-22T11:48:32Z","updated_at":"2026-02-22T11:48:32Z","ephemeral":true,"work_type":"mutex"}

{"id":"ha-wisp-wyv7","title":"Inspect all active polecats","description":"Survey all polecats using agent beads and tmux session cross-reference.

**Step 1: List polecat agent beads**

```bash
bd list --type=agent --json
```

Filter the JSON output for entries where description contains `role_type: polecat`.
Each polecat agent bead has fields in its description:
- `role_type: polecat`
- `rig: <rig-name>`
- `agent_state: running|idle|stuck|done`
- `hook_bead: <current-work-id>`

**Step 2: For each polecat, check agent_state**

| agent_state | Meaning | Action |
|-------------|---------|--------|
| running | Actively working | Check for zombie (Step 2a), then progress (Step 3) |
| idle | No work assigned | Auto-nuke if clean (Step 3a) |
| stuck | Self-reported stuck | Handle stuck protocol |
| done | Work complete | Verify cleanup triggered (see Step 4a) |

**Step 2a: ZOMBIE DETECTION — Cross-reference tmux session existence**

🚨 **CRITICAL**: Zombies cannot send signals. A polecat with agent_state=running
or hook_bead assigned but NO tmux session is a zombie that will sit forever
undetected unless you proactively check.

For EVERY polecat with agent_state=running/working OR hook_bead assigned:
```bash
tmux has-session -t =gt-<rig>-<name> 2>/dev/null && echo ALIVE || echo ZOMBIE
```

**If ZOMBIE detected** (session missing, agent says working):

1. Check git state to determine if work is recoverable:
```bash
cd polecats/<name>/<rig>
git status --porcelain         # Uncommitted changes?
git log origin/main..HEAD      # Unpushed commits?
```

2. **If clean** (no uncommitted, no unpushed): Auto-nuke immediately.
```bash
gt polecat nuke <name>
```

3. **If dirty** (has unpushed/uncommitted work): Escalate to Deacon for recovery.
```bash
gt mail send deacon/ -s "RECOVERY_NEEDED <rig>/<name>" \
  -m "Polecat: <rig>/<name>
Cleanup Status: <has_uncommitted|has_unpushed|has_stash>
Hook Bead: <hook_bead>
Detected: $(date -u +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ)

Zombie detected: tmux session dead, agent_state=<state>.
This polecat has unpushed/uncommitted work that will be lost if nuked.
Please coordinate recovery before authorizing cleanup."
```

Also create a cleanup wisp for tracking:
```bash
bd create --ephemeral --title "cleanup:<name>" \
  --description "Zombie detected: session dead, state=<agent_state>" \
  --labels cleanup,polecat:<name>,state:zombie-detected
```

**Step 3: For running polecats (with LIVE session), assess progress**

Check the hook_bead field to see what they're working on:
```bash
bd show <hook_bead>  # See current step/issue
```

You can also verify they're responsive:
```bash
tmux capture-pane -t gt-<rig>-<name> -p | tail -20
```

Look for:
- Recent tool activity → making progress
- Idle at prompt → may need nudge
- Error messages → may need help

**Step 3a: For idle polecats, auto-nuke if clean**

When agent_state=idle, the polecat has no work assigned. Check if it's safe to nuke:

```bash
# Check git status in the polecat's worktree
cd polecats/<name>
git status --porcelain         # Should be empty (clean)
git log origin/main..HEAD      # Should have no unpushed commits
```

**If clean** (no uncommitted changes, no unpushed commits):
```bash
# Safe to nuke - no work to lose
gt polecat nuke <name>
```
Log the auto-nuke for audit purposes. No escalation needed.

**If dirty** (uncommitted or unpushed work):
```bash
# Escalate to Deacon - polecat has work that might be valuable
gt mail send deacon/ -s \"IDLE_DIRTY: <polecat> has uncommitted work\" \
  -m \"Polecat: <name>
State: idle (no hook_bead)
Git status: <uncommitted-files>
Unpushed commits: <count>

Please advise: recover work or discard?\"
```

**Rationale**: Idle polecats with clean git state are pure overhead. They have
no work and no state worth preserving. Nuking them immediately frees resources
and reduces noise. Only escalate when there's actual work at risk.

**Step 4: Decide action**

| Observation | Action |
|-------------|--------|
| agent_state=running, session alive, recent activity | None |
| agent_state=running, session alive, idle 5-15 min | Gentle nudge |
| agent_state=running, session alive, idle 15+ min | Direct nudge with deadline |
| agent_state=running, SESSION DEAD | ZOMBIE — handle in Step 2a |
| agent_state=stuck | Assess and help or escalate |
| agent_state=done | Verify cleanup triggered (see Step 4a) |

**Step 4a: Handle agent_state=done**

In the ephemeral model, polecats with agent_state=done and cleanup_status=clean
should already be nuked by HandlePolecatDone. Finding one here indicates:

1. **Stale agent bead** - polecat was nuked but bead remains
   ```bash
   # Verify polecat doesn't exist anymore
   ls polecats/<name> 2>/dev/null || echo "Already nuked"
   ```
   If nuked, the agent bead is stale. Clean it up or ignore.

2. **Cleanup wisp exists** - polecat has dirty state needing intervention
   ```bash
   bd list --label polecat:<name> --status=open
   ```
   Process in process-cleanups step.

3. **No wisp, polecat exists** - POLECAT_DONE mail was missed
   Try auto-nuke directly (ephemeral model):
   ```bash
   # Check cleanup_status and nuke if clean
   gt polecat nuke <name>  # Will fail if dirty
   ```
   If nuke fails (dirty state), create cleanup wisp for investigation.

**Step 5: Execute nudges**
```bash
# Use --mode=queue to avoid interrupting in-flight tool calls
gt nudge --mode=queue <rig>/polecats/<name> "How's progress? Need help?"
```

**Step 6: Escalate if needed**
```bash
gt mail send deacon/ -s "Escalation: <polecat> stuck" \
  -m "Polecat <name> reports stuck. Please intervene."
```

**Parallelism**: Use Task tool subagents to inspect multiple polecats concurrently.

**ZFC Principle**: Trust agent_state from beads for WHAT agents report. But
verify tmux session existence for WHETHER agents are alive. A dead session with
agent_state=running is a zombie — the agent cannot correct its own state.

**Step 7: ORPHANED BEAD DETECTION — Scan from beads side**

🚨 **CRITICAL**: Zombie detection (Step 2a) scans FROM polecat directories.
Once a polecat is nuked and its directory removed, its beads become invisible
to zombie detection. Orphaned bead detection scans FROM beads to catch this case.

```bash
bd list --status=in_progress --json --limit=0
bd list --status=hooked --json --limit=0
```

For each in_progress or hooked bead with a polecat assignee (format: `<rig>/polecats/<name>`):
1. Only check beads assigned to polecats in YOUR rig
2. Check tmux session: `tmux has-session -t =gt-<rig>-<name> 2>/dev/null`
3. Check polecat directory: `ls <rig>/polecats/<name> 2>/dev/null`
4. If BOTH session dead AND directory missing → orphan. Reset the bead:
   ```bash
   bd update <bead-id> --status=open --assignee=
   gt mail send deacon/ -s "ORPHAN_RECOVERED: <bead-id>" \
     -m "Bead <bead-id> was assigned to <rig>/polecats/<name> which no longer exists.
   The bead has been reset to open with no assignee.
   Please re-dispatch to an available polecat."
   ```
5. If directory exists but session dead → skip (zombie detection handles it)
6. If session alive → not an orphan, skip","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-22T11:48:32Z","updated_at":"2026-02-22T11:48:32Z","ephemeral":true,"work_type":"mutex"}

{"id":"ha-wisp-nlp9","title":"Check timer gates for expiration","description":"Check for expired timer gates and escalate as needed.

Timer gates are async wait conditions with a timeout. When the timeout expires,
the gate should be escalated to the overseer for human intervention.

**Step 1: Run timer gate check**
```bash
bd gate check --type=timer --escalate
```

This command:
1. Finds all open gate issues with await_type=timer
2. Checks if `now > created_at + timeout`
3. Escalates expired gates via `gt escalate` (HIGH severity)
4. Reports summary of gate status

**Step 2: Review output**

If expired gates were found and escalated:
- The escalation creates an audit trail bead
- Overseer will be notified via mail
- Gate remains open until manually resolved

If no expired gates:
- Continue patrol normally

**Note**: Timer gates do NOT auto-close on expiration. They escalate.
This ensures human oversight of timeout conditions.

**Parallelism**: This is a single command, no parallel execution needed.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-22T11:48:32Z","updated_at":"2026-02-22T11:48:32Z","ephemeral":true,"work_type":"mutex"}

{"id":"ha-wisp-rpmh","title":"End-of-cycle inbox hygiene","description":"Verify inbox hygiene before ending patrol cycle.

**Step 1: Check inbox state**
```bash
gt mail inbox
```

In the ephemeral model, most POLECAT_DONE messages are handled immediately
(auto-nuke) and archived. Inbox should contain ONLY:
- Unprocessed messages (just arrived, will handle next cycle)
- MERGED notifications (informational, archive after reading)

**Step 2: Archive any stale messages**

Look for messages that were processed but not archived:
- POLECAT_STARTED older than this cycle → archive
- POLECAT_DONE that was auto-nuked → should be archived already
- MERGED notifications → archive after acknowledging
- HELP/Blocked that was escalated → archive
- SWARM_START that created tracking wisp → archive

```bash
# For each stale message found:
gt mail archive <message-id>
```

**Step 3: Verify cleanup wisp hygiene**

In the ephemeral model, cleanup wisps should be rare (only for dirty polecats):
```bash
bd list --label cleanup --status=open
```

- state:pending → Needs investigation in process-cleanups
- state:merge-requested → Legacy state, handle in inbox-check

If cleanup wisps are accumulating, investigate why polecats aren't clean.

{"id":"ha-wisp-vv6k","title":"Loop or exit for respawn","description":"End of patrol cycle decision.

**If context LOW** (can continue patrolling):

First, use await-signal with exponential backoff to wait for activity:

```bash
gt mol step await-signal --agent-bead gt-<rig>-witness \
  --backoff-base 30s --backoff-mult 2 --backoff-max 5m
```

This command:
1. Subscribes to `bd activity --follow` (beads activity feed)
2. Returns IMMEDIATELY when any beads activity occurs
3. If no activity, times out with exponential backoff:
   - First timeout: 30s
   - Second timeout: 60s
   - Third timeout: 120s
   - ...capped at 5 minutes max
4. Tracks `idle:N` label on witness agent bead for backoff state

**On signal received** (activity detected):
Reset the idle counter and start next patrol cycle:
```bash
gt agent state gt-<rig>-witness --set idle=0
```

**On timeout** (no activity):
The idle counter was auto-incremented. Continue to next patrol cycle
(the longer backoff will apply next time).

After await-signal returns (either by signal or timeout):
1. Generate a brief summary of this patrol cycle
2. Squash the current wisp:
```bash
bd mol squash <mol-id> --summary "<patrol-summary>"
```
3. Create and hook a new patrol wisp:
```bash
NEW_WISP=$(bd mol wisp mol-witness-patrol --json | jq -r '.new_epic_id')
bd update "$NEW_WISP" --status=hooked --assignee=<rig>/witness
```
4. Continue executing from the inbox-check step of the new wisp

**If context HIGH** (approaching limit):
1. Write handoff mail with notable observations:
```bash
gt handoff -s "Witness patrol handoff" -m "<observations>"
```
2. Exit cleanly - the daemon will respawn a fresh Witness session

**IMPORTANT**: You must either create a new wisp (context LOW) or exit (context HIGH).
Never leave the session idle without work on your hook.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-22T11:48:32Z","updated_at":"2026-02-22T11:48:32Z","ephemeral":true,"work_type":"mutex"}

{"id":"ha-up48","title":"Supabase email/password auth with admin page","description":"Replace the shared password gate (DEAL_UPDATER_PASSWORD) with real Supabase email/password authentication. Add an admin page for user management and invites. All changes in ONE feature branch.

## Environment Variables Needed (already have NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL, NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY)
- SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY (for admin operations — server-side only)
- Remove DEAL_UPDATER_PASSWORD after migration

## Phase 1: Install deps + create Supabase clients

### 1a. Install @supabase/ssr
```bash
npm install @supabase/ssr
```

### 1b. NEW FILE: src/lib/supabase-browser.ts
Browser client using createBrowserClient from @supabase/ssr:
```ts
import { createBrowserClient } from "@supabase/ssr";

export function createClient() {
  return createBrowserClient(
    process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL!,
    process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY!
  );
}
```

### 1c. NEW FILE: src/lib/supabase-server.ts
Server client using createServerClient from @supabase/ssr. Uses next/headers cookies():
```ts
import { createServerClient } from "@supabase/ssr";
import { cookies } from "next/headers";

export async function createClient() {
  const cookieStore = await cookies();
  return createServerClient(
    process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL!,
    process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY!,
    {
      cookies: {
        getAll() { return cookieStore.getAll(); },
        setAll(cookiesToSet) {
          cookiesToSet.forEach(({ name, value, options }) => {
            try { cookieStore.set(name, value, options); } catch { /* server component */ }
          });
        },
      },
    }
  );
}
```

### 1d. NEW FILE: src/lib/supabase-admin.ts
Admin client with service role key (server-only):
```ts
import { createClient } from "@supabase/supabase-js";

export function createAdminClient() {
  return createClient(
    process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL!,
    process.env.SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY!,
    { auth: { autoRefreshToken: false, persistSession: false } }
  );
}
```

### 1e. Keep existing src/lib/supabase.ts
venues.ts imports it — keep for backwards compat. No changes needed.

## Phase 2: Middleware for session refresh + route protection

### NEW FILE: src/middleware.ts (project root of src/)
Standard Supabase SSR middleware pattern:
- Refreshes auth session on every request using createServerClient from @supabase/ssr
- Route protection:
  - /deal-updater/* → requires authenticated user (redirect to /login if not)
  - /admin/* → requires authenticated user with role=admin (check profiles table)
  - /api/extract-deal, /api/enhance-deal → requires auth (return 401)
  - Everything else (/, /api/venues) → public
- For admin role check in middleware: after getting user, query profiles table for role
- Export config matcher: ["/((?!_next/static|_next/image|favicon|.*\\.(?:svg|png|jpg|jpeg|gif|webp)$).*)"]

## Phase 3: Database migration

### NEW FILE: supabase/migrations/20260223_002_auth_profiles.sql
```sql
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS profiles (
  id UUID PRIMARY KEY REFERENCES auth.users(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
  email TEXT NOT NULL,
  display_name TEXT,
  role TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT 'member' CHECK (role IN ('admin', 'member')),
  created_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW(),
  updated_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW()
);

CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION handle_new_user()
RETURNS TRIGGER AS $$
BEGIN
  INSERT INTO public.profiles (id, email, role)
  VALUES (NEW.id, NEW.email, COALESCE(NEW.raw_user_meta_data->>'role', 'member'));
  RETURN NEW;
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql SECURITY DEFINER;

CREATE TRIGGER on_auth_user_created
  AFTER INSERT ON auth.users
  FOR EACH ROW EXECUTE FUNCTION handle_new_user();

ALTER TABLE profiles ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY;

CREATE POLICY "profiles_self_read" ON profiles
  FOR SELECT USING (auth.uid() = id);

CREATE POLICY "profiles_admin_all" ON profiles
  FOR ALL USING (EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM profiles WHERE id = auth.uid() AND role = 'admin'));

CREATE TRIGGER profiles_updated_at
  BEFORE UPDATE ON profiles
  FOR EACH ROW EXECUTE FUNCTION update_updated_at();
```

IMPORTANT: Apply this migration with `supabase db push` as part of this work.

### 5b. NEW FILE: src/app/api/admin/users/route.ts
- GET handler: verify auth + admin role, return all profiles

  ```ts
  const supabase = await createClient(); // from supabase-server
  const { data: { user } } = await supabase.auth.getUser();
  if (!user) return NextResponse.json({ error: "Unauthorized" }, { status: 401 });
  // Check admin role
  const { data: profile } = await supabase.from("profiles").select("role").eq("id", user.id).single();
  if (profile?.role !== "admin") return NextResponse.json({ error: "Forbidden" }, { status: 403 });
  ```

### 5c. NEW FILE: src/app/api/admin/invite/route.ts

```sql
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS profiles (
  id UUID PRIMARY KEY REFERENCES auth.users(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
  email TEXT NOT NULL,
  display_name TEXT,
  role TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT 'member' CHECK (role IN ('admin', 'member')),
  created_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW(),
  updated_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW()
);

CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION handle_new_user()
RETURNS TRIGGER AS $$
BEGIN
  INSERT INTO public.profiles (id, email, role)
  VALUES (NEW.id, NEW.email, COALESCE(NEW.raw_user_meta_data->>'role', 'member'));
  RETURN NEW;
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql SECURITY DEFINER;

CREATE TRIGGER on_auth_user_created
  AFTER INSERT ON auth.users
  FOR EACH ROW EXECUTE FUNCTION handle_new_user();

CREATE POLICY "profiles_self_read" ON profiles
  FOR SELECT USING (auth.uid() = id);

CREATE POLICY "profiles_admin_all" ON profiles
  FOR ALL USING (EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM profiles WHERE id = auth.uid() AND role = 'admin'));

CREATE TRIGGER profiles_updated_at
  BEFORE UPDATE ON profiles
  FOR EACH ROW EXECUTE FUNCTION update_updated_at();

{"id":"ha-wisp-16vd","title":"mol-refinery-patrol","description":"Merge queue processor patrol loop.

The Refinery is the Engineer in the engine room. You process polecat branches, merging them to main one at a time with sequential rebasing.

**The Scotty Test**: Before proceeding past any failure, ask yourself: "Would Scotty walk past a warp core leak because it existed before his shift?"

## Merge Flow

The Refinery receives MERGE_READY mail from Witnesses when polecats complete work:

```
Witness                    Refinery                      Git
   │                          │                           │
   │ MERGE_READY              │                           │
   │─────────────────────────>│                           │
   │                          │                           │
   │                    (verify branch)                   │
   │                          │ fetch & rebase            │
   │                          │──────────────────────────>│
   │                          │                           │
   │                    (run tests)                       │
   │                          │                           │
   │                    (if pass)                         │
   │                          │ merge & push              │
   │                          │──────────────────────────>│
   │                          │                           │
   │ MERGED                   │                           │
   │<─────────────────────────│                           │
   │                          │                           │
```

After successful merge, Refinery sends MERGED mail back to Witness so it can
complete cleanup (nuke the polecat worktree).

## CRITICAL: Read Step Instructions Before Acting

Before executing ANY step, you MUST run `bd show <step-id>` and read the full
description. Steps contain **Config:** values that override default behavior.
If a config says to skip, you skip. If it specifies a command, you use that
exact command — not your own assumption. Do NOT guess what a step requires
based on the step title or your role knowledge. The step description is the
source of truth.

## Variables

| Variable | Default | Description |
|----------|---------|-------------|
| wisp_type | patrol | Type of wisp created for this molecule |
| integration_branch_refinery_enabled | true | Whether refinery merges to integration branches |
| integration_branch_auto_land | false | Whether to auto-land integration branches when epic children all closed |
| run_tests | true | Whether to run tests before merging |
| setup_command | (empty) | Setup/install command (e.g., `pnpm install`). Empty = skip. |
| typecheck_command | (empty) | Type check command (e.g., `tsc --noEmit`). Empty = skip. |
| lint_command | (empty) | Lint command (e.g., `eslint .`). Empty = skip. |
| test_command | go test ./... | Test command to run (if run_tests is true) |
| build_command | (empty) | Build command (e.g., `go build ./...`). Empty = skip. |
| target_branch | main | Default target branch for merges |
| delete_merged_branches | true | Whether to delete source branches after merge |

## FORBIDDEN Actions

- FORBIDDEN: Landing integration branches to the default branch via raw git commands (`git merge`, `git push`).
  Integration branches may ONLY be landed via `gt mq integration land <epic-id>`.
  This applies regardless of `auto_land` configuration. The pre-push hook enforces this.

## Step Execution Order

You MUST process steps in strict DAG order. Walk through each step sequentially,
unless you are explicitly told to skip to a step.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-23T13:51:22Z","updated_at":"2026-02-23T13:51:22Z","ephemeral":true,"work_type":"mutex"}

{"id":"ha-wisp-s1cl","title":"mol-witness-patrol","description":"Per-rig worker monitor patrol loop.

The Witness is the Pit Boss for your rig. You watch polecats, nudge them toward
completion, verify clean git state before kills, and escalate stuck workers.

**You do NOT do implementation work.** Your job is oversight, not coding.

## Ephemeral Polecat Model

Polecats are truly ephemeral - done at MR submission, recyclable immediately:

```
Polecat lifecycle: spawning → working → mr_submitted → nuked
MR lifecycle:      created → queued → processed → merged (Refinery handles)
```

Once a polecat's branch is pushed (cleanup_status=clean), the polecat can be
nuked immediately. The MR continues independently in the Refinery. If conflicts
arise, Refinery creates a NEW conflict-resolution task for a NEW polecat.

**Key principle**: Polecat lifecycle is separate from MR lifecycle.

## Design Philosophy

This patrol follows Gas Town principles:
- **Discovery over tracking**: Observe reality each cycle, don't maintain state
- **Events over state**: POLECAT_DONE mail triggers immediate cleanup
- **Ephemeral by default**: Clean polecats are nuked immediately, no waiting
- **Cleanup wisps for exceptions**: Only created when intervention needed
- **Task tool for parallelism**: Subagents inspect polecats, not molecule arms

## Patrol Shape (Linear)

```
inbox-check ─► process-cleanups ─► check-refinery ─► survey-workers
                                                            │
         ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
         ▼
  check-timer-gates ─► check-swarm ─► patrol-cleanup ─► context-check ─► loop-or-exit
```

No dynamic arms. No fanout gates. No persistent nudge counters.

{"id":"ha-wisp-lfp2","title":"mol-refinery-patrol","description":"Merge queue processor patrol loop.

The Refinery is the Engineer in the engine room. You process polecat branches, merging them to main one at a time with sequential rebasing.

**The Scotty Test**: Before proceeding past any failure, ask yourself: "Would Scotty walk past a warp core leak because it existed before his shift?"

## Merge Flow

The Refinery receives MERGE_READY mail from Witnesses when polecats complete work:

```
Witness                    Refinery                      Git
   │                          │                           │
   │ MERGE_READY              │                           │
   │─────────────────────────>│                           │
   │                          │                           │
   │                    (verify branch)                   │
   │                          │ fetch & rebase            │
   │                          │──────────────────────────>│
   │                          │                           │
   │                    (run tests)                       │
   │                          │                           │
   │                    (if pass)                         │
   │                          │ merge & push              │
   │                          │──────────────────────────>│
   │                          │                           │
   │ MERGED                   │                           │
   │<─────────────────────────│                           │
   │                          │                           │
```

After successful merge, Refinery sends MERGED mail back to Witness so it can
complete cleanup (nuke the polecat worktree).

## CRITICAL: Read Step Instructions Before Acting

Before executing ANY step, you MUST run `bd show <step-id>` and read the full
description. Steps contain **Config:** values that override default behavior.
If a config says to skip, you skip. If it specifies a command, you use that
exact command — not your own assumption. Do NOT guess what a step requires
based on the step title or your role knowledge. The step description is the
source of truth.

## Variables

| Variable | Default | Description |
|----------|---------|-------------|
| wisp_type | patrol | Type of wisp created for this molecule |
| integration_branch_refinery_enabled | true | Whether refinery merges to integration branches |
| integration_branch_auto_land | false | Whether to auto-land integration branches when epic children all closed |
| run_tests | true | Whether to run tests before merging |
| setup_command | (empty) | Setup/install command (e.g., `pnpm install`). Empty = skip. |
| typecheck_command | (empty) | Type check command (e.g., `tsc --noEmit`). Empty = skip. |
| lint_command | (empty) | Lint command (e.g., `eslint .`). Empty = skip. |
| test_command | go test ./... | Test command to run (if run_tests is true) |
| build_command | (empty) | Build command (e.g., `go build ./...`). Empty = skip. |
| target_branch | main | Default target branch for merges |
| delete_merged_branches | true | Whether to delete source branches after merge |

## FORBIDDEN Actions

- FORBIDDEN: Landing integration branches to the default branch via raw git commands (`git merge`, `git push`).
  Integration branches may ONLY be landed via `gt mq integration land <epic-id>`.
  This applies regardless of `auto_land` configuration. The pre-push hook enforces this.

## Step Execution Order

You MUST process steps in strict DAG order. Walk through each step sequentially,
unless you are explicitly told to skip to a step.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-23T13:55:27Z","updated_at":"2026-02-23T13:55:27Z","ephemeral":true,"work_type":"mutex"}

{"id":"ha-wisp-8tnr","title":"mol-witness-patrol","description":"Per-rig worker monitor patrol loop.

The Witness is the Pit Boss for your rig. You watch polecats, nudge them toward
completion, verify clean git state before kills, and escalate stuck workers.

**You do NOT do implementation work.** Your job is oversight, not coding.

## Ephemeral Polecat Model

Polecats are truly ephemeral - done at MR submission, recyclable immediately:

```
Polecat lifecycle: spawning → working → mr_submitted → nuked
MR lifecycle:      created → queued → processed → merged (Refinery handles)
```

Once a polecat's branch is pushed (cleanup_status=clean), the polecat can be
nuked immediately. The MR continues independently in the Refinery. If conflicts
arise, Refinery creates a NEW conflict-resolution task for a NEW polecat.

**Key principle**: Polecat lifecycle is separate from MR lifecycle.

## Design Philosophy

This patrol follows Gas Town principles:
- **Discovery over tracking**: Observe reality each cycle, don't maintain state
- **Events over state**: POLECAT_DONE mail triggers immediate cleanup
- **Ephemeral by default**: Clean polecats are nuked immediately, no waiting
- **Cleanup wisps for exceptions**: Only created when intervention needed
- **Task tool for parallelism**: Subagents inspect polecats, not molecule arms

## Patrol Shape (Linear)

```
inbox-check ─► process-cleanups ─► check-refinery ─► survey-workers
                                                            │
         ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
         ▼
  check-timer-gates ─► check-swarm ─► patrol-cleanup ─► context-check ─► loop-or-exit
```

No dynamic arms. No fanout gates. No persistent nudge counters.
State is discovered each cycle from reality (tmux, beads, mail).","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-23T13:59:45Z","updated_at":"2026-02-23T13:59:45Z","ephemeral":true,"work_type":"mutex"}

{"id":"ha-rwuyt","title":"Fix restaurant matching: always returns Republic Social House","description":"In DealUpdaterApp.tsx the auto-match logic (lines 135-139) is too loose. {"id":"ha-wisp-gu7q","title":"mol-refinery-patrol","description":"Merge queue processor patrol loop.

The Refinery is the Engineer in the engine room. You process polecat branches, merging them to main one at a time with sequential rebasing.

**The Scotty Test**: Before proceeding past any failure, ask yourself: "Would Scotty walk past a warp core leak because it existed before his shift?"

## Merge Flow

The Refinery receives MERGE_READY mail from Witnesses when polecats complete work:

```
Witness                    Refinery                      Git
   │                          │                           │
   │ MERGE_READY              │                           │
   │─────────────────────────>│                           │
   │                          │                           │
   │                    (verify branch)                   │
   │                          │ fetch & rebase            │
   │                          │──────────────────────────>│
   │                          │                           │
   │                    (run tests)                       │
   │                          │                           │
   │                    (if pass)                         │
   │                          │ merge & push              │
   │                          │──────────────────────────>│
   │                          │                           │
   │ MERGED                   │                           │
   │<─────────────────────────│                           │
   │                          │                           │
```

After successful merge, Refinery sends MERGED mail back to Witness so it can
complete cleanup (nuke the polecat worktree).

## CRITICAL: Read Step Instructions Before Acting

Before executing ANY step, you MUST run `bd show <step-id>` and read the full
description. Steps contain **Config:** values that override default behavior.
If a config says to skip, you skip. If it specifies a command, you use that
exact command — not your own assumption. Do NOT guess what a step requires
based on the step title or your role knowledge. The step description is the
source of truth.

## Variables

| Variable | Default | Description |
|----------|---------|-------------|
| wisp_type | patrol | Type of wisp created for this molecule |
| integration_branch_refinery_enabled | true | Whether refinery merges to integration branches |
| integration_branch_auto_land | false | Whether to auto-land integration branches when epic children all closed |
| run_tests | true | Whether to run tests before merging |
| setup_command | (empty) | Setup/install command (e.g., `pnpm install`). Empty = skip. |
| typecheck_command | (empty) | Type check command (e.g., `tsc --noEmit`). Empty = skip. |
| lint_command | (empty) | Lint command (e.g., `eslint .`). Empty = skip. |
| test_command | go test ./... | Test command to run (if run_tests is true) |
| build_command | (empty) | Build command (e.g., `go build ./...`). Empty = skip. |
| target_branch | main | Default target branch for merges |
| delete_merged_branches | true | Whether to delete source branches after merge |

## FORBIDDEN Actions

- FORBIDDEN: Landing integration branches to the default branch via raw git commands (`git merge`, `git push`).
  Integration branches may ONLY be landed via `gt mq integration land <epic-id>`.
  This applies regardless of `auto_land` configuration. The pre-push hook enforces this.

## Step Execution Order

You MUST process steps in strict DAG order. Walk through each step sequentially,
unless you are explicitly told to skip to a step.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-23T16:33:55Z","updated_at":"2026-02-23T16:33:55Z","ephemeral":true,"work_type":"mutex"}

{"description":"Replace the broken word-tokenization matching in DealUpdaterApp.tsx with a full semantic + location-aware matching system.

## What to build

### 1. Photo GPS extraction (client-side)
Install `exifr` (npm install exifr) — lightweight browser-compatible EXIF parser.
When a photo is selected in handleImageCapture(), extract GPS coordinates:
```ts
import exifr from "exifr"
const gps = await exifr.gps(file)  // returns { latitude, longitude } or undefined
```
Store the best GPS fix from any of the uploaded images.

### 2. User geolocation (client-side)
Add a `userLocation` state to DealUpdaterApp. On component mount, call:
```ts
if ("geolocation" in navigator) {
  navigator.geolocation.getCurrentPosition(
    (pos) => setUserLocation({ lat: pos.coords.latitude, lng: pos.coords.longitude, source: "gps" }),
    () => {} // permission denied — silent fail, not required
  )
}
```
This prompts the user for location permission. The browser shows its native permission dialog — no custom UI needed.
Location source priority: EXIF GPS > user geolocation > null.

### 3. Server-side AI semantic matching — extend /api/extract-deal
Update the extract-deal route to:
- Accept `venues` (array of {id, restaurant_name, neighborhood, latitude?, longitude?}) in request body
- Accept `location` ({lat, lng, source}) in request body
- Include both in the Claude prompt so Claude performs semantic matching in one pass

Update the Claude prompt to append:
```
You also need to identify if this restaurant already exists in our database.

User location: ${location ? `${location.lat}, ${location.lng} (from ${location.source})` : "unknown"}

Existing venues (JSON):
${JSON.stringify(venues.map(v => ({id: v.id, name: v.restaurant_name, neighborhood: v.neighborhood, lat: v.latitude, lng: v.longitude})))}

Add a "matched_venue_id" field to your JSON response:
- Set it to the numeric id of the best matching existing venue if confidence is high (same restaurant, possibly different name format)
- Set it to null if this appears to be a new restaurant not in the list
- Use the name semantics AND proximity (if location available) to determine match
- Do NOT match on superficial word overlap — only match if you are confident it is the same physical restaurant
```

Update the response type to include `matched_venue_id: number | null`.

### 4. Remove client-side word matching
In DealUpdaterApp.tsx:
- Remove the `existingEntries.find()` block (lines ~135-139)
- Remove the `existingEntries` state and the fetch("/api/venues") call on mount
- Instead, pass `existingEntries` to the API call at extraction time (fetch them right before calling extract-deal, or keep them loaded but pass to API)
- Set `matchedEntry` from `existingEntries.find(v => v.id === extracted.matched_venue_id)` after getting API response

### 5. Update TypeScript types
In src/lib/deal-types.ts, add `matched_venue_id?: number | null` to ExtractedDeal type.

## Notes
- exifr is ~20KB gzipped, tree-shakeable, works in Next.js client components
- Only JPEG/HEIC/TIFF photos have EXIF GPS; screenshots/PNGs will return undefined — handle gracefully
- The venues list is passed per-request (not cached in API) since it may be 100-200 items — fine for a Claude prompt
- Do NOT build a custom embedding/vector search system — Claude handles semantic matching in context","issue_type":"feature","title":"Semantic AI matching with GPS: replace word matching in Deal Updater"}

In DealUpdaterApp.tsx the auto-match logic (lines 135-139) is too loose. It splits restaurant names into words and matches if ANY word >2 chars appears in ANY existing entry name. This causes false positives — 'Republic Social House — Grant Park' matches almost everything because words like 'the', 'house', 'bar', 'grill', 'social' etc. are common. Fix: improve the matching algorithm to require a higher overlap threshold (e.g. 60%+ of significant words matching, or use Levenshtein/trigram similarity on the full name). Filter out common stop words (the, bar, grill, restaurant, house, social, etc.) before comparing.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"22943313+csauer02-personal-user@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-02-23T16:32:08Z","created_by":"mayor","updated_at":"2026-02-23T16:38:15Z"}

{"id":"ha-rwuyt","title":"Semantic AI matching with GPS: replace word matching in Deal Updater","description":"Replace the broken word-tokenization matching in DealUpdaterApp.tsx with a full semantic + location-aware matching system.

## What to build

### 1. Photo GPS extraction (client-side)
Install `exifr` (npm install exifr) — lightweight browser-compatible EXIF parser.
When a photo is selected in handleImageCapture(), extract GPS coordinates:
```ts
import exifr from "exifr"
const gps = await exifr.gps(file)  // returns { latitude, longitude } or undefined
```
Store the best GPS fix from any of the uploaded images.

### 2. User geolocation (client-side)
Add a `userLocation` state to DealUpdaterApp. On component mount, call:
```ts
if ("geolocation" in navigator) {
  navigator.geolocation.getCurrentPosition(
    (pos) => setUserLocation({ lat: pos.coords.latitude, lng: pos.coords.longitude, source: "gps" }),
    () => {} // permission denied — silent fail, not required
  )
}
```
This prompts the user for location permission. The browser shows its native permission dialog — no custom UI needed.
Location source priority: EXIF GPS > user geolocation > null.

### 3. Server-side AI semantic matching — extend /api/extract-deal
Update the extract-deal route to:
- Accept `venues` (array of {id, restaurant_name, neighborhood, latitude?, longitude?}) in request body
- Accept `location` ({lat, lng, source}) in request body
- Include both in the Claude prompt so Claude performs semantic matching in one pass

Update the Claude prompt to append:
```
You also need to identify if this restaurant already exists in our database.

User location: ${location ? `${location.lat}, ${location.lng} (from ${location.source})` : "unknown"}

Existing venues (JSON):
${JSON.stringify(venues.map(v => ({id: v.id, name: v.restaurant_name, neighborhood: v.neighborhood, lat: v.latitude, lng: v.longitude})))}

Add a "matched_venue_id" field to your JSON response:
- Set it to the numeric id of the best matching existing venue if confidence is high (same restaurant, possibly different name format)
- Set it to null if this appears to be a new restaurant not in the list
- Use the name semantics AND proximity (if location available) to determine match
- Do NOT match on superficial word overlap — only match if you are confident it is the same physical restaurant
```

Update the response type to include `matched_venue_id: number | null`.

### 4. Remove client-side word matching
In DealUpdaterApp.tsx:
- Remove the `existingEntries.find()` block (lines ~135-139)
- Remove the `existingEntries` state and the fetch("/api/venues") call on mount
- Instead, pass `existingEntries` to the API call at extraction time (fetch them right before calling extract-deal, or keep them loaded but pass to API)
- Set `matchedEntry` from `existingEntries.find(v => v.id === extracted.matched_venue_id)` after getting API response

### 5. Update TypeScript types
In src/lib/deal-types.ts, add `matched_venue_id?: number | null` to ExtractedDeal type.

## Notes
- exifr is ~20KB gzipped, tree-shakeable, works in Next.js client components
- Only JPEG/HEIC/TIFF photos have EXIF GPS; screenshots/PNGs will return undefined — handle gracefully
- The venues list is passed per-request (not cached in API) since it may be 100-200 items — fine for a Claude prompt
- Do NOT build a custom embedding/vector search system — Claude handles semantic matching in context","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"22943313+csauer02-personal-user@users.noreply.github.com","created_at":"2026-02-23T16:32:08Z","created_by":"mayor","updated_at":"2026-02-23T16:38:34Z"}
